description: What each modern C++ standard introduced and why it beats the old way: C++11/14 (auto, range-for, lambdas, move semantics, nullptr, smart pointers, constexpr, threads, make_unique) and C++17/20/23 (structured bindings, optional/variant, if constexpr, filesystem, concepts, ranges, coroutines, modules, the spaceship operator, expected). Every card is answerable from the primer.
primer:
## What this set covers
The evolution of C++ by standard, framed as 'what changed and why it's better than the old way'. Every card traces back to this page.

## C++11 — the 'modern C++' turning point
The first major revision in over a decade; it reshaped idioms.
- **`auto`**: deduces a variable's type, replacing verbose names (`auto it = m.begin();` instead of the full iterator type).
- **Range-based for**: `for (const auto& x : v)` replaces index/iterator boilerplate and off-by-one bugs.
- **Move semantics**: rvalue references (`T&&`) and move constructors **transfer** a resource cheaply instead of deep-copying — enables move-only types.
- **`nullptr`**: a typed null (own type `std::nullptr_t`) that can't be mistaken for the integer `0`, unlike `NULL`.
- **Smart pointers**: `unique_ptr`, `shared_ptr`, `weak_ptr` replaced raw `new`/`delete` ownership and the broken `auto_ptr` — automatic, exception-safe RAII.
- **`constexpr`**: compile-time evaluation of constants/functions (C++11 limited functions to a single return).
- **Threading**: `std::thread`, `std::mutex`, `std::atomic`, `std::future`/`std::async` + a memory model, replacing platform-specific APIs.
- **Lambdas**: inline anonymous callables, finally making `<algorithm>` ergonomic (vs hand-written functors).
- **`= default` / `= delete`**: explicitly keep or forbid special members (`= delete` makes a type non-copyable cleanly).

## C++14 — polish on C++11
- **`std::make_unique`**: the factory C++11 forgot (C++11 only had `make_shared`); creates a `unique_ptr` with no visible `new`, exception-safe — the recommended way.
- **Generic lambdas**: `[](auto a, auto b){...}` — `auto` parameters make a lambda a templated callable.
- **Relaxed `constexpr`**: constexpr functions gained loops, local variables and multiple statements.

## C++17 — quality-of-life
- **Structured bindings**: `auto [a, b] = pair;` unpacks tuples/pairs/structs into named variables (great for `for (auto& [k, v] : m)`).
- **`std::optional<T>`**: represents 'maybe a value' without sentinels (`-1`) or nullable pointers.
- **`std::variant`**: a type-safe tagged union holding one of a fixed set of types (vs a raw `union`).
- **`if constexpr`**: compile-time branching in templates — only the taken branch is instantiated (replaces SFINAE/tag-dispatch tricks).
- **`std::filesystem`**: a portable standard API for paths and files.

## C++20 — a major release
- **Concepts**: named, checkable constraints on template parameters (`template <std::integral T>`), giving **clearer errors** than SFINAE/`enable_if`.
- **Ranges**: composable, **lazy** view pipelines over whole ranges, e.g. `v | std::views::filter(...) | std::views::transform(...)` — no begin/end pairs, no temporaries.
- **Coroutines**: suspendable/resumable functions (`co_await`, `co_yield`, `co_return`) for async and generators in sequential style.
- **Modules**: `import` instead of textual `#include` — faster builds, no macro leakage, real encapsulation.
- **Spaceship `<=>`**: one three-way comparison; `= default` generates all six relational operators at once.
- **`consteval`**: an 'immediate function' that *must* run at compile time (stronger than `constexpr`).

## C++23 — rounding out C++20
- **`std::expected<T,E>`**: a value-or-**error** return type — unlike `optional` (which only signals 'empty'), it carries *why* it failed, an exception-free error channel.
- **`std::print`/`std::println`**: formatted printing on top of C++20's `std::format`.
- Plus **`std::mdspan`**, multidimensional `operator[]`, **deducing `this`**, and `import std;` standard-library module support. The theme is ergonomics.

## The thread
Each standard removes a sentinel, a piece of boilerplate, or a manual-management hazard: optional kills magic return values, `<=>` kills hand-written comparisons, smart pointers kill manual `delete`, ranges/concepts make generic code readable.
